conf-proc-title-group.conf-full-title: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Systems: Theories and Applications (SITA)
conf-proc-title-group.conf-normalized-title: Intelligent Systems: Theories and Applications (SITA), 2025 International Conference on

conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Systems: Theories and Applications (SITA)

title-group.article-title: ML for Insurance Risk Prediction: Models, Metrics, and Insights with a Graph-Causal-Transformer Approach

institution-wrap.institution: Laboratory of information technology and Modeling, Faculty of Sciences Ben M'Sik, Hassan II University

permissions.copyright-statement: 979-8-3315-5989-2 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: Nearly all insurance operations are dependent on risk prediction, the accuracy and fairness of which is paramount. Traditional models of underwriting and risk assessment generally fail to exhibit the complexity and interrelationships associated with an interconnected portfolio. This paper proposes the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) an innovative machine learning architecture that employs graph neural networks, causal inference, and transformer based attention to allow for a more holistic and interpretable consideration of insurance risk. Whereas conventional models assume policyholders represent independent risks, the GCT augments its models of risk by directly modeling relationships between policyholders, assets, and their context, such as supply chains or relative distance. It also distinguishes true causal drivers (or influences) from false correlations. The GCT is evaluated using a very large scale auto insurance dataset augmented with alternative datasets. The GCT model performed better than Generalised Linear Models, XGBoost, Graph Neural Networks and Actuarial Transformers depending upon the performance metric employed, namely AUC, Gini, loss-ratio uplift and fairness. The GCT provides a high degree of interpretability through SHAP based local explanations and causal path insights, therefore supporting those firms who need to comply with emerging regulations associated with the rise of AI. The findings of this study demonstrate that the GCT provides predictive performance benefits while enhancing transparency and governance whilst making it an important candidate for future algorithmic underwriting applications in regulated insurance markets.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: One of the most important things in the insurance industry is the ability to correctly predict the risks. Insurers use Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) and regression-based resilience (and related) actuarial methods as their main tools to measure the risk of policyholders. Besides the recognition of the great support among regulators of GLM frameworks, insurers appreciate the perspective during the auditing process, but GLMs are restricted in linearity having constraints on combining features and static relationships which are tending to err with the complex nature of the insurance data available. He insurance sector is undergoing a digital transformation and the insurers can now access a wide variety of new data streams - telematics data on your driving; The Internet of Things with all the sensors coming from your smart home (or smart device); satellite images; and even social media and behavioral data [1]. The availability of the new data creates a wealth of opportunities as well as challenges for the insurers.

Machine learning algorithms like gradient boosting, neural nets, and transformers have been found to be more powerful than the conventional frameworks to make predictions. Machine learning top three barriers are the lack of interpretability, lack of fairness protections, and difficulty in modeling dynamic (or relational) structures of risk [2].

The predictive modeling used in the current predictive methods have a major drawback which is that the treatment of policyholders or assets as single entities dominates the most part of the modeling practice.Risk is not treated this way and often has interconnectedness; proximity, vendors, social connections, and supply chain. In fact, after a natural disaster, hundreds of policyholders will suffer simultaneous losses. In observance of this risk interconnectedness, we intend to emphasize risk interdependence in The Protection Gap Model - an interconnected framework of this nature could create a better consideration of risk both at the level of individual assets and across vehicles/assets within geographies[3]. These relational patterns are ignored in traditional tabular models because systems thinking cannot anticipate systemic exposures or cascading losses. Another contributing factor is the increasing scrutiny, demands for model governance, and transparency, especially given new regulations like the EU AI Act. Increasingly, decision-makers are responsible for explainability, fairness, and accountability when using algorithmic decision making [4], especially with high-stakes applications.

In light of these knowledge gaps, this article proposes a new architecture, the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), as we formalize three breakthroughs in modern machine learning: graph neural networks to model relationships between entities, causal inference to separate true drivers of risk vs. coincident correlations, and transformer attentional mechanisms to propagate feature interactions. Our overall modeling framework, will help enhance not only predictive performance but also fairness, interpretability and ease of adoption in operational insurance systems. This paper begins with a review of related work in traditional actuarial modeling and recent advances in ML for insurance applications. We then present the GCT methodology in detail, describing its core components and training procedures. Next, we evaluate the model on a real-world dataset and benchmark its performance against industry-standard baselines. Following this, we discuss the practical implications of our findings, including interpretability, regulatory compliance, and scalability. Finally, we conclude by summarizing key insights and outlining avenues for future research.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Related Works
sec.p: Risk prediction in insurance has advanced quickly in the past 20 years, moving away from traditional actuarial methods to a future with more data and algorithmically driven approaches. This section summarises the broad classes of models that have been part of this evolution and discuss how they are useful for the various challenges this paper seeks to address.

The backbone of insurance risk modeling has also been the Generalised Linear Models (GLM) and Generalised Additive Models (GAMs). The strength of these models is transparency and interpretability which is good for regulators and audits [5]. They are still popular for base pricing, reserving and risk segmentation. The linear/additive assumptions prevent GLMs or GAMs from capturing the complex interactions and non-linearities in high-dimension actuarial data [6].

The new growth of tree-based ensemble methods, namely Random Forests and Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDTs) have shown to be a substantial improvement in predictive power over linear models. Tree-based ensemble methods do not require any specification of non-linear relationships or characterisation of feature interactions [7]. XGBoost has emerged as a popular tool among insurers primarily due to its efficiency, accuracy, and tendency to outperform many similar models in terms of overfitting risk. Nevertheless, these models operate under the independence assumption and neglect structured, relational, or timing dependencies. Each data point is still treated independently even if the underlying relationship structure is a concern in areas like fraud detection or catastrophe modeling [8].

More recently, deep learning architectures, such as fully connected networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), have been applied to insurance challenges in the document classification, image inspection, and voice triage realms [9], [10]. While these models can identify representations of unstructured or semi-structured information very well, this black-box architecture creates many challenges socially (usability and acceptability) and by regulators who embrace a more fiduciary duty and want some degree of model transparency.

With increasing demands for accuracy and transparency to support decision making, researchers have recently introduced the Actuarial Transformer (AT) [11] as a specific application of a transformer architecture, developed for insurance scenarios and has been specifically customized for tabular insurance data. AT models utilize attention and they are powerful because they allow modelers to capture relationships in global feature space simultaneously, while enabling some interpretability given researchers can evaluate the attention weights or SHAP explanations. Still, these models make the same i.i.d. independence assumption and they treat observations with structured relationships independently in the same general spirit as the deep learning models before. To progress the state of relational dependencies, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have gained traction for use in insurance applications (again, specifically in fraud detection and supply-chain risk modeling [12]). GNNs use graphs to model data such that nodes are entities and edges represent relationships (e.g., co-claims, proximity, shared service providers). Historically, researchers have shown GNNs outperform traditional methods in identifying patterns of fraud that occur in clusters or rings [13]. However, existing methods, including GNNs, generally lack the capacity for causal reasoning, which is particularly important for fairness and generalizability.

Alongside the development and application of graph learning methods, causal inference has gained traction in the insurance context, particularly in fairness-aware modeling and reducing bias. As examples, double machine learning, structural equation modeling, and causal discovery algorithms (e.g., NOTEARS) have been proposed and operationalized to help distinguish between spurious correlations and causal drivers of risk [14], [15]. The distinction between correlation and other variables or causal factors is important because, in cases where regulators specify that insurers cannot take certain variables into consideration (e.g., gender, zip code), insurers must demonstrate model behavior that goes beyond simply being statistically significant. However, there is no single model that currently combines adversarial structures, causal reasoning, and deep attention mechanisms in a humanly interpretable model for risk prediction. This paper seeks to fill that gap by proposing the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), a new model that we have developed which attempts to remedy the limitation of current methods and provide an adaptable, interpretable, regulation-friendly framework for modern insurers.

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Methodology
sec.p: In this section, we present the proposed Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) architecture to capture the complex, relational, and dynamic nature of insurance risk. The GCT combines graph learning, causal inference, and attentionbased deep learning in a single architecture. The GCT incorporates local interactions (e.g., individual policyholder behavior) and global interactions (e.g., systemic or cascading risks across networks); it also leverages interpretability and fairness within regulatory constraints.

We are representing the portfolio as a graph $\mathrm{G}=(V, E)$ with $\vert\mathrm{V}\vert=\mathrm{N}$ nodes (policyholders/assets/actors) and $\vert\mathrm{E}\vert$ edges (relations). Node features $\mathrm{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d 0 X}$.with $d$, the graph has depth $L$, and attention heads $H$. These symbols are used to denote the complexity of the model below.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Graph-Causal-Transformer (Gct) Architecture
sec.p:
The Guided Collaborative Transformer (GCT) architecture consists of three modules that are tightly coupled. Each module defines a unique facet of the model's predictive and interpretive capabilities:
Figure1.
Graph-causal-transformer(gct)overview.

list-item.p:
Transformer-Based Encoder-Decoder: The predictive engine is based on a transformer architecture that has been specifically adapted to incorporate graph-aware tabular data. In the encoder phase, raw node embeddings based solely on the underlying graph structure are augmented by causal relevance scores and passed through a multi-head selfattention layer. This allows the model to prioritize and combine information from both the local node features and the relational context of the node itself. Furthermore, the general architecture supports multitask learning modes, and thus, allows the model to simultaneously learn jointly to predict several targets that are important in insurance operations:
•
Claim severity, trained using Tweedie loss (appropriate for zero-inflated, right-skewed data). •
Claim frequency, modeled with Poisson or Negative Binomial loss. •
Churn propensity, framed as a binary classification task using cross-entropy loss.
We augment token/node embeddings with $\tilde{h}_{\mathcal{V}}^{(k)}$ before attention.
The multi-head self-attention component adds explainability by clarifying the most relevant features and relationships for each prediction.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Training Pipeline
sec.p: The process of training GCT entails four distinct phases with the goal of getting the most out of data while promoting model robustness, interpretability, and fairness:

Data Preprocessing: The training pipeline consumes five years of multimodal insurance (policy and claims) data including structured records produced during the insurance transaction (e.g., policy and claims data), unstructured text (e.g., claim adjuster notes), and sensor-generated time-series data such as vehicle telemetry and smart home devices, as well as external context datasets (e.g., weather patterns, crime indices, and economic indicators). The records are normalized, encoded, aligned temporally, and represented as graphs.

Self-Supervised Pretraining: Before supervised learning, the model is pretrained through self-supervised tasks that include a means of obtaining structural or semantic information via edge prediction (making predictions using missing or future links between nodes), and node feature reconstruction (predicting node attributes of a node based on the node's neighborhood). This feature is more likely to allow the model to generalize and is a way to helpthe potential data sparsity and other issues.

Self-Supervised Pretraining: The model is initially trained with self-supervised learning frameworks followed by supervised learning. The self-supervised learning tasks were centered on the extraction of structural and semantic features, for instance, edge prediction (i.e., predicting missing or new edges between nodes) and node feature reconstruction (i.e., predicting node features from neighborhood distributions). This phase opens up the path for more generalization from the data while resolving problems such as data sparsity.
Figure 2.
Gct training workflow.

sec.label: C.
sec.title: Experimental Results
sec.p: Describing the experimental design to evaluate the performance of the new Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) model, this section includes the characteristics of the dataset used, baseline models, and the evaluation results presented not only in one but in several important metrics. The model will be measured against its predictive accuracy, fairness, explainability, and operational consideration.

sec.title: Dataset
sec.p:
The experiments were conducted on the CASD (Comprehensive Auto Insurance Simulation Dataset) Auto Insurance Claims Dataset, a large-scale dataset, created in collaboration with the CAS (Casualty Actuarial Society), the dataset is known as a benchmark for risk modeling in the auto insurance domain, fraud detection, and telemetry pricing, used widely in both academia and the industry for benchmarking purposes. OpenWeatherMap provided the weather data, while Eurostat provided the economic indicators; these and other publicly available data sources were used to further enrich the data.
•
Size: 1.2 million unique policy records, including approximately 80,000 claims. •
Features: 180 structured tabular features (e.g., demographics, vehicle info, policy terms), 15 timeseries telematics fields (e.g., acceleration, speed, braking patterns), and geospatial metadata (e.g., region codes, traffic risk zones). •
Data Splitting: A stratified 80-10-10 train-validationtest split was applied. To ensure statistical stability, all metrics are averaged over three runs with different random seeds. •
Data Source: CASD (Comprehensive Auto Insurance Simulation Dataset) by the Casualty Actuarial Society, supplemented with OpenWeatherMap and Eurostat datasets for external feature enrichment.

sec.title: Baseline Models
sec.p:
The To benchmark the performance of the GCT model, we compare it against four commonly used models in the insurance industry:
GLM (Generalized Linear Model): The actuarial standard, included for baseline interpretability.
XGBoost: A popular gradient boosting method, known for its performance and flexibility.
Actuarial Transformer: A transformer-based model optimized for structured tabular data, incorporating attentionbased feature learning [3].
Graph Neural Network (GNN): A relational learning model that leverages graph topology but lacks explicit causal or attention mechanisms.

Each model was trained on the same dataset and evaluated using five metrics: AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve), Gini coefficient, uplift in loss ratio over the GLM baseline, fairness (measured as demographic parity gap), and explanation latency using SHAP (in milliseconds).
Table I Benchmark results on casd
Model AUC (Claims) Gini Loss-Ratio Uplift Demographic Gap ↓ SHAP Latency (ms)
GLM 0.78 0.56 Baseline 0.00 5
XGBoost 0.85 0.70 +5.1% 0.07 12
Actuarial Transformer 0.88 0.76 +7.3% 0.06 55
GNN 0.86 0.72 +6.0% 0.05 23
GCT (proposed model) 0.91 0.80 +10.4% 0.02 61

Observations:
Predictive Performance: GCT achieves the highest AUC (0.91) and Gini coefficient (0.80), indicating superior discriminatory power in identifying high-risk policyholders.
Fairness: The demographic parity gap is significantly lower in GCT (0.02) compared to other ML models, suggesting that the causal module successfully mitigates bias introduced by correlated demographic features.
Business Impact: GCT improves portfolio loss ratio by 10.4 % relative to GLM, outperforming all baselines.
Explainability: While GCT incurs slightly higher explanation latency due to its layered architecture, it remains within acceptable operational thresholds ($<100 ~\text{ms}$), and the added interpretability from causal-path tracing offers substantial governance value.

These results demonstrate that the proposed architecture provides a well-balanced trade-off between accuracy, fairness, and explainability-making it suitable for high-stakes, regulated insurance environments.

sec.label: D.
sec.title: Computational Complexity & Optimizations.
sec.p: Per batch, GNN message passing is $\mathcal{O}(M d)$ per layer; transformer self-attention is $\mathcal{O}\left(n^{2} d\right)$ for token length nnn; causal regularization $\mathcal{O}(N d)-\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ depending on the estimator. Optimizations: mini-batch graph sampling (GraphSAGE/Cluster-GCN), sparse/linear attention, parameter sharing, mixed precision, quantization/distillation for edge/real-time use, and GPU batching for SHAP. This keeps GCT deployable for daily scoring while reserving heavier causal audits for batch windows.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This part initially discusses the consequences of the experimental findings by highlighting the benefits of the proposed Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT) alongside some of the practical issues in its deployment in the insurance field.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Strengths of the Gct Model
sec.p: The GCT model is enriched with several benefits that give it an edge over the previous methods adopted in the literature. These benefits are further discussed in terms of relational modeling, fairness, and operationalization.

Relational Reasoning: The GCT model takes into account the relationships that exist among different conditional independence structures and develops a latent structure that acknowledges the overlap between policyholders, assets, and contextual variables as one single unit. This was a feature that made it superior compared to traditional univariate models that consider all risks as independent observations. The GCT model is the most appropriate for cases where there are systematic exposures (e.g., regional weather, supply chain risks, fraud rings). In such instances, the GCT model will be instrumental in modeling both cascading and accumulation risks, which is a great advantage in portfolios that are increasingly sensitive to climate and exposed to cyber risks.

Fairness and Regulatory Compliance: Introducing a causal inference module enables GCT to analyze and potentially remove some reliance on proxy variables that may incur observed bias. This has potential benefits in terms of fairness of predictions, particularly regarding protected attributes such as age, gender or location. The causal modeling capability of the model allows it to be better positioned for conforming to any emerging regulations related to AI, such as the EU AI Act and NAIC Model Law 687, which both require proof of protections against algorithmic discrimination.

Operational Readiness: Even though I anticipate that GCT's architecture will be somewhat complicated as far as its logic and structure are concerned, it was conceived to be scalable to deployment. Dynamic graph partitioning enables modifications to the graph structure that are caused by new claims, policy changes, or telemetry signals to take place in a localized manner and be communicated quickly. Besides, well-organized model documentation (e.g., model cards) with integrated explainability has the potential of assisting governance both internally and externally which, in turn, will be the approval of the model in the regulated insurance environment.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Practical Considerations
sec.p: Despite GCT having its fair share of advantages, its introduction in the actual world is laden with various hiccups. These challenges need to be solved first if GCT is to perform as expected.

Data Latency and Integration: A significant portion of the graph's success depends on the timely ingestion and integration of event data, such as claims and telematics streams. To keep the graph's freshness and prediction relevance, a reliable data pipeline with real-time stream processing (e.g., Apache Kafka or similar) would be required.

Computational Efficiency: The computation of the inference within GCT is more complicated than in the case of traditional ensemble methods due to the use of attention mechanisms and graph embeddings. Nevertheless, there are some opportunities for performance optimization (e.g., parameter sharing, attention pruning, and model quantization), but there will still be a certain amount of extra computing resources required. In the case of high-throughput applications such as usage-based insurance and $\mu$-underwriting, we can easily use the lightweight versions of GCT which have the reduced data on-edge or are accelerated with GPUs.

Interpretability and User Trust: The success of any ML system in insurance is not only based on the system's performance but also on user trust. These GCT explore built-in explainability tools, but their worth is contingent upon appropriate usability. Visualization tools (like SHAP-based dashboards, causal path diagrams, etc.) can be co-designed with actuaries and underwriters, - given that model outputs are familiar for accessible interpretation and can be consistent with domain expertise. We must include frontline users in this process to gain widespread acceptance.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This research put forward a new architecture of risk prediction in insurance namely the Graph-Causal-Transformer (GCT), an amalgam of graph-based learning, causal inference, and transformer-based attention mechanisms, which serves as an interpretable model. The policyholders are traditional models that isolate, GCT instead exploits the relational aspect of an insurance portfolio and identifies conditional and structural dependencies not only between the entities but also between the supply chains, geographical clusters, and behavioral networks. As GCT provides the structural information, it is able to incorporate causal reasoning, thus increasing predictive accuracy as well as promoting fairness and regulatory accountability.

We built a large-scale and industry-relevant dataset to carry out empirical experiments, and the results showed that the GCT outperforms several baseline methods, including GLMs, gradient boosting, graph neural networks, and actuarial transformers, on different aspects-predictive performance (AUC and Gini) for business value (uplift on loss ratios), and for ethical practice (differential disparity in demographic variables). Thus, we have gone a long way towards making the first, second, and third comparisons stronger without losing the potential for the model's explainability, which, in the regulated insurance field, is a crucial feature.

Streaming data. The explainability component of the model relies on SHAP values and causal path tracing. This explainability layer is also available for the use of internal governance, regulatory audits, and underwriting trust.

In terms of future research and applied improvement, GCT creates many paths forward. For example, GCT can extend the architecture into multi-line or reinsurance situations, use foundation models to comprehend document level understanding, or apply reinforcement learning to provide dynamic pricing options. As the insurance industry algorithmically assisted underwriters, models like GCT represent an essential leap to make risk management more intelligent, potentially adaptive, and responsible.

ref.mixed-citation: S. Singireddy, INTelLIGENT RISK AND PROTECTION Revolutionizing Auto, Home, Life, and Travel Insurance with Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. CANEDA GLOBAL JOURNAL GROUP.
R. R. Danda, Z. Yasmeen, and K. K. Maguluri, AI-Driven Healthcare Transformation: Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and Neural Networks in Insurance and Wellness Programs. JEC PUBLICATION.

Y. Yao, “Bayesian network model structure based on binary evolutionary algorithm,” PeerJ Comput. Sci., vol. 9, p. e1466, 2023.
K. Kuppan, D. B. Acharya, and B. Divya, “Foundational AI in Insurance and Real Estate: A Survey of Applications, Challenges, and Future Directions,” IEEE Access, 2024.

A. Kesharwani and P. Shukla, “FFDM- GNN: A Financial Fraud Detection Model using Graph Neural Network,” in 2024 International Conference on Computing, Sciences and Communications (ICCSC), IEEE, 2024, pp. 1–6.
